summary: DeVault advocates for git's built-in email workflow (git send-email, format-patch, git am) as superior to GitHub-style pull requests. He walks through the toolchain, configuration tips, and patch review etiquette, drawing from his own contribution to git 2.18.0. He argues email is standardized, federated, and infinitely customizable, while acknowledging that popular email clients make the workflow harder than it should be. Rather than abandoning email, he announces sr.ht's approach: embrace the email-driven model while building tools to lower the barrier to entry. He points to the Linux kernel as proof the model scales to the largest projects on earth.
key_points: Git was designed around email-driven development, and its built-in tools (format-patch, send-email, am) form a complete patch workflow
Email is standardized, federated, and open—letting developers customize every layer without proprietary lock-in
Bottom-posting and inline reply style enable precise, contextual code review directly within email threads
The Linux kernel's massive development pace proves email-driven workflows scale far beyond what centralized platforms handle
Rather than abandoning email for GitHub-style PRs, sr.ht aims to make email workflows more accessible with better tooling
money_quotes: Email isn't as sexy as GitHub (and its imitators), but it has several advantages over the latter.
Email is standardized, federated, well-understood, and venerable.

Given that most popular email clients these days are awful and can't handle basic tasks like "sending email" properly, I strongly recommend this tool over attempting to send format-patch's output yourself.
A secret these email clients have kept from you is that you are, in fact, permitted by the mail RFCs to edit the sender's message as you please when replying.
I don't think that the solution to these problems is to leave these powerful tools hanging in the wind and move to less powerful models like GitHub's pull requests.
A change is made to the Linux kernel an average of 7 times per hour, constantly.
All of this works without any centralization or proprietary software involved. We should embrace this!
key_insight: Git's email-driven workflow is more powerful, open, and scalable than centralized pull request models—the answer to its rough edges is better tooling, not abandoning it.
